items.q: What is the central innovation of Cubism?
items.correct: Showing an object from multiple viewpoints simultaneously on a flat surface.
items.why: Students take the name literally. Cubism is named for facet-like fragmentation, but it isn't about cubes - it's about breaking the single Renaissance viewpoint into many at once.

items.q: What were Impressionist painters primarily trying to capture?
items.correct: The fleeting effects of light and atmosphere at a particular moment.
items.misconception: Their feelings or first impressions of a scene.
items.why: Students take 'impression' as personal feeling. Impressionism was about visual perception - how light actually appears on surfaces, painted quickly outdoors before light changed.

items.q: What is the rule of thirds?
items.correct: A guideline placing key elements along lines or intersections of a 3x3 grid.
items.misconception: A strict mathematical rule that must never be broken.
items.why: Students treat it as inviolable. It's a useful HEURISTIC for engaging compositions - many great works ignore it. Centred compositions can be powerful too; the rule is a starting point, not a law.

items.q: How many resolved artworks does an IBDP HL exhibition require minimum?
items.why: It's 8. “10” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Whose 1976 essay critiques the white cube gallery?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: O'Doherty. “Krauss” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Documenta first open in Kassel?
items.why: The real figure is 1955 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
